items.q: How do headlands and bays form on a discordant coastline?
items.correct: Less resistant rock (e.g. clay) is eroded faster, forming bays; more resistant rock (e.g. chalk) is eroded slower, left protruding as headlands — perpendicular bands of geology.
items.misconception: Headlands form where the sea bends round to attack the land.
items.random_wrong: Headlands and bays form because of differences in sea temperature.
items.why: The pattern is set by geology (differential erosion of rock bands meeting the coast at right angles), not by water behaviour. Concordant coasts (parallel bands) produce coves, not bays.

items.q: What is the sequence of headland erosion from crack to stump?
items.correct: Crack to cave to arch to stack to stump — waves widen cracks into caves, two caves meet to form an arch, the arch collapses leaving a stack, and the stack erodes to a stump.
items.misconception: Stacks form where rocks fall from the cliff into the sea.
items.random_wrong: Stacks form when the sea level rises and floods a hill.
items.why: It's a sequence of erosion at the same site, not random rockfall or sea level rise. Each landform is the predecessor of the next, with hydraulic action and abrasion driving the progression.

items.q: What is the difference between constructive and destructive waves?
items.correct: Constructive waves have strong swash and weak backwash, building beaches; destructive waves have weak swash and strong backwash, dragging sediment offshore and eroding cliffs.
items.misconception: Big waves are destructive and small waves are constructive.
items.random_wrong: Constructive waves build sandcastles and destructive waves wash them away.
items.why: It's the swash-to-backwash balance, not size, that classifies waves. Tall, plunging waves are destructive; long, spilling waves are constructive. Wave frequency also matters.

items.q: Approximate length of the Jurassic Coast?
items.why: It's 95 miles. “20 miles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a famous natural arch on the coast?
items.why: Correct answer: Durdle Door. “Old Harry” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year the Jurassic Coast was made a World Heritage Site?
items.why: Exact answer: 2001. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
